header.title: CloudClips
header.subtitle: Serverless short-video platform with AI content moderation and auto-generated captions

summary.plain_english: A full-stack video sharing platform where users upload short clips that are automatically transcoded into two resolutions, scanned for inappropriate content by machine learning, and served globally via CDN — all without a single always-on server. Every component from infrastructure to the React frontend is defined in code and deployed automatically on every push.

motivation.quote: I wanted to go beyond tutorial CRUD apps and build something with real distributed systems complexity — async pipelines, event-driven architecture, AI integration, and a custom CI/CD setup with zero stored credentials. Video platforms touch nearly every hard problem in cloud engineering at once.

diagram.alt: Architecture diagram showing browser uploading to S3 via presigned URL, triggering an EventBridge event, Lambda transcoding with FFmpeg, SNS fan-out to Rekognition and Transcribe, and CloudFront serving the results

architecture.flow_summary: Browser → API Gateway → presign-upload Lambda → S3 (presigned PUT) → EventBridge → SQS → transcode Lambda (FFmpeg) → SNS → Rekognition + Transcribe → DynamoDB → CloudFront CDN

items: Videos upload at full connection speed without touching a server — presigned S3 URLs let the browser PUT directly to S3 — Lambda is never in the upload path, so throughput scales to the user's network, not a server limit

Every uploaded video is automatically transcoded into 720p and 360p — FFmpeg running on an ARM64 Lambda with a static binary layer — viewers get adaptive quality with no always-on transcoding infrastructure and no cost at idle

All content is scanned for inappropriate material before it goes live — AWS Rekognition Video processes each clip asynchronously and blocks publishing if any label exceeds the confidence threshold — moderation is enforced by the pipeline, not by trust

Captions are generated automatically for every published video — Amazon Transcribe produces VTT subtitle files that ship alongside the video via CloudFront — accessibility is built into the pipeline, not an optional afterthought

The custom domain, TLS certificate, and DNS record are all self-managing — Lambda-backed CDK Custom Resources call the Cloudflare API at deploy time — the certificate and CNAME stay correct after every deploy with no manual console steps

The entire platform deploys from a single command with no stored credentials — 10 CDK stacks and a GitHub Actions pipeline using OIDC federation — any developer can recreate the full environment from scratch in one push

CDK cross-stack circular dependency: S3 buckets can't trigger Lambda directly across stacks without each stack needing a reference to the other. Solved by enabling EventBridge on the upload bucket — the Storage stack stays unaware of Processing, and the Processing stack creates the EventBridge rule independently.

ACM certificate with Cloudflare DNS: CDK's built-in Certificate construct only supports Route 53 for DNS validation. I wrote a Lambda-backed CloudFormation Custom Resource that calls the Cloudflare API to upsert the validation CNAME and polls ACM until the certificate is issued — runs automatically on every deploy.

CloudFront OAC bucket policies: When CDK imports an S3 bucket by ARN (required to break the CDN ↔ Storage circular dependency), it can't automatically add the OAC-required bucket policy. The deploy script applies this as a post-deploy step — a constraint I only discovered after the first deploy returned 403s.

GitHub Actions missing VITE_* env vars: The original deploy workflow ran the frontend build with no environment variables set, meaning the deployed app would silently connect to nothing. Fixed by fetching live values from CloudFormation outputs mid-pipeline and writing them to $GITHUB_ENV before the build step.

FFmpeg on Lambda sizing: Running FFmpeg inside a Lambda function requires careful memory and ephemeral storage configuration — too little and the process OOMs or runs out of /tmp space mid-transcode. Landed on 2 GB RAM and 2 GB /tmp after testing, which comfortably handles clips up to several minutes.
